book.title: Playing to Win
book.author: A.G. Lafley and Roger L. Martin

chapters.title: Start With a Real Winning Aspiration

summaryBullets: Defining what winning means before making strategic choices creates clearer judgment.
Strategy sharpens when the aim is explicit instead of vague.
This matters because clear winning aspirations improve tradeoffs because they narrow what matters.
A common mistake is treating growth, quality, and general improvement as enough of a strategy.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the company has goals but no clear definition of what winning would look like.
A practical starting move is to state what winning means, separate aspiration from generic improvement, and use the aspiration to guide choices.
keyTakeaways: Defining what winning means before making strategic choices creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: state what winning means
separate aspiration from generic improvement
use the aspiration to guide choices
review whether the team shares the same target

summaryBlocks.text: Start With a Real Winning Aspiration explains the main pattern behind the chapter in a direct and practical way. One core idea is this: Defining what winning means before making strategic choices creates clearer judgment. Another key idea is this: Strategy sharpens when the aim is explicit instead of vague.

In practice, these ideas matter because clear winning aspirations improve tradeoffs because they narrow what matters. A useful starting move is to state what winning means, then separate aspiration from generic improvement. Repeated use builds better judgment over time because small decisions start aligning with longer term goals.

summaryBullets: Making deliberate choices about market, customer, channel, and geography creates clearer judgment.
Strategy gets real when opportunity is narrowed into a winnable space.
This matters because better choices come from focus because resources are always constrained.
A common mistake is trying to play everywhere that looks attractive.
The pattern becomes easier to see when resources spread thin because the field was never narrowed.
A practical starting move is to choose the segment explicitly, name what you will not pursue, and check whether the space is winnable.
keyTakeaways: Making deliberate choices about market, customer, channel, and geography creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: choose the segment explicitly
name what you will not pursue
check whether the space is winnable
align the rest of the plan to the chosen field

chapters.title: Build the Capabilities the Strategy Requires

summaryBullets: Developing the capabilities that make the strategy executable creates clearer judgment.
A strategy survives only when the organization can perform the logic it claims.
This matters because capabilities matter because aspirations without support collapse under pressure.
A common mistake is announcing a strategy without building the skills or systems it depends on.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the plan sounds coherent until delivery exposes the missing capability.
A practical starting move is to name the required capabilities, invest in the few that matter most, and connect capability building to the strategy.
keyTakeaways: Developing the capabilities that make the strategy executable creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.

Coherence matters because each choice should support the next one.
